title: Authoring a standard — sections of clauses, and what good looks like
when_to_read: When you create a standard (docType='standard'), edit one, or are asked to look at a codebase and craft/distill standards for a team. Covers what makes a good standard and a good clause, the multi-section shape a standard should take (Rule + Rationale + Scope, mirroring the reference standard mindset-prod/memex-building-itself/std-1), WHERE a team's rules already live (CLAUDE.md, the user's memory file, READMEs, other .md, lint/CI configs, the code), and the full create_doc -> add_section(clauses) x N -> get_doc -> add_clause / edit_clause / delete_clause flow.
body:
## When to write a standard

A **standard** is a durable rule the team and its agents agree to follow — the thing you refuse to get wrong twice. Write one when a decision or hard-won lesson should bind *future* work, not just the Spec in front of you. If it only governs the current work, it's a Spec decision, not a standard.

## A standard is sections of clauses (not prose)

Unlike a Spec (free prose sections), a standard is an exhaustive partition into **clauses**. Each section is an ordered list of clauses, and the clause is the addressable unit:

- A **clause** is ONE self-contained aspect — a single rule, definition, or example. NOT a compound paragraph. "Encrypt at rest AND rotate keys quarterly" is two clauses.
- Each clause gets an allocate-once handle `cl-N` — stable, and never reused even after a delete. You cite and edit clauses by that handle.
- A section's content IS its clauses joined in order; there is no separate prose body.

## A standard has MORE than one section — do not collapse it to a bare Rule

A standard is not a lone list of rules. It is several typed sections, each a list of clauses, mirroring the reference standard `mindset-prod/memex-building-itself/std-1` (read it — it is the shape to copy). The section types:

- **Rule** (required) — the normative clauses: the actual obligations, each independently checkable.
- **Rationale** (default — include it) — *why* the rule exists: what it unblocks, what breaks without it, which decision it resolves. This is what lets an agent reason about the rule at an edge case the clauses didn't spell out.
- **Scope** (default — include it) — the exact surfaces, layers, or packages the rule applies to, and what it explicitly excludes. Without a Scope the agent loop over-applies the rule where it doesn't belong, or skips it where it does.
- **Description** (optional) — a one-line framing when the title alone isn't self-evident.
- **Vocabulary** (optional) — only when the standard fixes naming / handle conventions.

**The default shape is Rule + Rationale + Scope.** A standard that is just a Rule section is under-specified: a rule with no Scope gets mis-applied, and a rule with no Rationale can't be extended safely. Add Description / Vocabulary only when they earn their place.

## What makes a good clause

- **One aspect.** If "what does this clause say?" needs the word "and", split it.
- **Self-contained.** Readable on its own, without the neighbouring clauses.
- **Checkable where possible.** A rule a reviewer or test could verify ("every public route has a smoke test") beats vague intent ("we value testing").
- **Imperative and specific.** Name the trigger and the obligation.

## What makes a good standard

- A **Rule** section whose clauses are each independently true and verifiable.
- A **Rationale** a cold reader understands in one pass: why this binds future work, and what breaks without it.
- A **Scope** that says what's in and what's out, so the rule isn't over-applied.
- More than one section: never a bare Rule. Default to Rule + Rationale + Scope.
- No duplication of an existing standard — link it instead.
- When in doubt, copy the shape of `mindset-prod/memex-building-itself/std-1`.

## Distilling standards from an existing codebase

A common first task: a developer installs the Memex MCP on an existing repo and asks "look at my codebase and craft some standards for me." The rules a team already lives by are usually written down somewhere — find them first, then turn each into a standard of clauses. Where to look, in priority order:

1. **CLAUDE.md files** (repo root, nested packages, and `.claude/` rules files) — the richest source. They exist precisely to tell an agent the team's rules: coding style, architecture boundaries, do/don't, workflows. Most lines are already clause-shaped ("always X", "never Y") — treat each as a candidate clause.
2. **The user's memory file** (the agent's `memory.md` / persistent memory) — durable preferences, corrections, and "always/never" feedback the user has accumulated. High-signal, and often the *un*-written rules.
3. **README.md + docs** (`docs/`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, ADRs, runbooks) — setup invariants and "how we do things here."
4. **Other `.md` files** across the repo — design notes, per-package guidelines.
5. **The codebase itself** — lint/format configs (eslint, prettier, ruff), CI workflows, test conventions, directory layout, naming patterns. A rule the code consistently follows or a linter enforces is a real standard even if nobody wrote it in prose.

### Turning findings into standards

- **Group by theme, not by source file.** One standard per coherent topic ("Error handling", "API response shape", "Testing"), each authored as Rule + Rationale + Scope sections of clauses — never one giant "misc" standard, and never a lone Rule with no Scope or Rationale.
- **One rule -> one clause.** Split compound rules; keep the team's own wording where you can. You are capturing, not rewriting.
- **Put the why in the Rationale, the where in the Scope.** When you distil a rule from CLAUDE.md or the code, capture the obligation as a Rule clause AND the reason/boundary as Rationale/Scope clauses — don't drop them.
- **Cite the source** in a clause where it helps ("enforced by eslint rule X", "per CLAUDE.md").
- **Confirm before mass-creating.** Surface the proposed standards (titles + a clause or two each) and let the user approve/trim before authoring — don't dump 30 standards unprompted.
- **Don't invent.** Only promote rules the codebase/docs actually evidence; if something is ambiguous, ask rather than guess.

Then author each via the create_doc -> add_section(clauses) flow below, adding a Rule, a Rationale, and a Scope section.

## Worked example — author a standard end to end

Standards are authored at the clause grain, one `add_section` per section type. The full loop:

1. Create it (born with NO body section):
   `create_doc({ memex, title: "Smoke tests are mandatory", docType: "standard", purpose: "<one-line why>" })`
   -> returns `std-N`. A standard is born sectionless; content arrives as clauses.

2. Add the **Rule** section as clauses (passing `content` to a standard is REJECTED — standards take `clauses`):
   `add_section({ ref: "<ns>/<memex>/standards/std-N", sectionType: "rule", title: "Rule", clauses: [`
   `  "Every change touching a user-facing surface adds a smoke test.",`
   `  "Smoke runs against the deployed host after each deploy.",`
   `  "A green local suite is necessary but not sufficient." ] })`
   -> creates section `s-1` with clauses `cl-1, cl-2, cl-3`; the response echoes the new `cl-N` handles.

3. Add the **Rationale** section (the why) — also clauses:
   `add_section({ ref: "<...>/standards/std-N", sectionType: "rationale", title: "Rationale", clauses: [`
   `  "Local suites pass against mocks; only a deployed smoke catches wiring, secrets, and migration drift.",`
   `  "Catching a broken deploy at the tail of the pipeline is far cheaper than a customer report." ] })`

4. Add the **Scope** section (the where) — also clauses:
   `add_section({ ref: "<...>/standards/std-N", sectionType: "scope", title: "Scope", clauses: [`
   `  "Applies to every public HTTP route and every Cloud Run deploy.",`
   `  "Excludes internal scripts and one-off backfills." ] })`

5. Read it to discover clause handles across all sections:
   `get_doc({ ref: "<...>/standards/std-N" })`
   -> each clause renders inline with its handle, e.g. `[cl-2] Smoke runs against the deployed host after each deploy.`

6. Append a clause to a section (omit `position` to append; pass it to insert):
   `add_clause({ ref: "<...>/standards/std-N/sections/s-1", body: "Promote to prod only once int smoke is green." })`
   -> `cl-N`.

7. Edit a clause by its handle (section content regenerates; the `cl-N` identity is kept):
   `edit_clause({ ref: "<...>/standards/std-N/clauses/cl-2", body: "Smoke runs automatically at the tail of every deploy." })`

8. Delete a clause (soft-delete; `cl-N` is frozen and siblings are NOT renumbered):
   `delete_clause({ ref: "<...>/standards/std-N/clauses/cl-3" })`

That is the whole loop: create -> add_section(clauses) for Rule, Rationale, and Scope -> get_doc(read handles) -> add_clause / edit_clause / delete_clause. Standards are never edited with `update_section` — it is blocked on standards; edit the clauses instead.
